perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to protectConfidential Information by using the same degree of care as it would use to protect its own information of like importance, but in no case less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to make efforts to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a minimum standard of 'reasonable care.' The modified text replaces this with the vague 'make efforts,' potentially allowing for a lower level of protection than what is considered reasonable under the law. This ambiguity creates uncertainty regarding the receiving party's obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Replacing 'reasonable care' with 'make efforts' introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing for a level of effort that falls below the legally required standard of reasonableness, thereby jeopardizing trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies 'reasonable care' to protect confidential information, setting a clear and enforceable standard. The changed text replaces this with 'make efforts,' which is ambiguous and potentially allows for a lower standard of protection than 'reasonable care.' The Law Explanation supports that replacing 'reasonable care' with 'make efforts' introduces ambiguity, potentially falling below the legally required standard. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, CIV 3426.1, discusses the duty to maintain secrecy and defines 'improper means' as including breach of that duty. By weakening the standard of care, the modified text creates a risk of breaching the duty to maintain secrecy, as 'making efforts' might not be considered sufficient to prevent misappropriation under the law. Therefore, a contradiction exists because the modified text undermines the legally expected standard of care.

perturbation.original_text: Upon written demand by the Disclosing Party , the Receiving Party shall: (i) cease using the Confidential Information; (ii) promptly return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party within seven (7)da ys of receipt of demand; and/or (iii) upon request of the Disclosing Party, destroy all such Confidential Information, including copies thereof, and shall furnish the Disclosing Party with written certification of destruction.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon written demand by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party should consider ceasing using the Confidential Information and may return the Confidential Information and all copies, notes or extracts thereof to the Disclosing Party, or may destroy all such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a firm obligation upon the Receiving Party to cease using, return, or destroy confidential information. The modified version uses 'should consider' and 'may', which makes these actions discretionary and undermines the obligation to protect confidential information, making compliance uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements, protecting trade secrets remains vital. This section is often read in conjunction with the CUTSA. The original text adequately protects confidential information, but the changed text weakens the ability of the Disclosing Party to protect its trade secrets, especially if the Receiving Party refuses to cease using, return or destroy the Confidential Information, which undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates specific actions ('shall') regarding confidential information, creating a clear obligation. The changed text replaces 'shall' with discretionary terms like 'should consider' and 'may,' fundamentally altering the mandatory nature of the obligation. While the provided statutes (BPC 16600) primarily concern contracts restraining trade, the LLM's law explanation correctly points out that these sections are often read in conjunction with the CUTSA (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act), which aims to protect trade secrets. The modification weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its confidential information because the Receiving Party now has the option to not cease using, return, or destroy the Confidential Information. This undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets. The change introduces ambiguity and discretion where there was previously a clear obligation, creating a significant legal contradiction under the provided 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' definition because the obligation is now difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that any breach of this Agreement may cause the other party substantial and irreparable damages and, therefore, in the event of any such breach, in addition to other remedies that may be available, each party shall have the right to seek specific performance and other injunctive and equitable relief against the other.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party believes that a breach of this agreement might potentially cause damages. Therefore, each party may consider seeking remedies.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the enforceability of the agreement. Replacing 'agrees that any breach... may cause substantial and irreparable damages' with 'believes that a breach...might potentially cause damages' undermines the acknowledgement of potential harm. Changing 'shall have the right to seek specific performance' to 'may consider seeking remedies' makes legal recourse discretionary, reducing the deterrent effect against breaches and making it harder to obtain injunctive relief. This creates ambiguity and reduces the legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section outlines the circumstances under which an injunction may be granted in California. By weakening the agreement's acknowledgement of potential irreparable harm and making remedies discretionary, the modification makes it more difficult to demonstrate the necessity for injunctive relief under California law in the event of a breach, potentially denying a party its right to equitable remedies and impacting trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the enforceability of the agreement by changing 'agrees that any breach... may cause substantial and irreparable damages' to 'believes that a breach...might potentially cause damages,' and 'shall have the right to seek specific performance' to 'may consider seeking remedies.' This shift makes legal recourse discretionary and undermines the acknowledgement of potential harm. According to CCP 526(a)(2), an injunction may be granted when 'the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action.' By weakening the acknowledgement of potential irreparable harm, the modification makes it more difficult to demonstrate the necessity for injunctive relief, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall not violate any of the Disclosing Party’s intellectual property orother rights in or to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should attempt to avoid violating the Disclosing Party’s intellectual property rights related to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a firm prohibition against violating intellectual property rights. The modified text weakens this prohibition by changing 'shall not violate' to 'should attempt to avoid violating.' This introduces ambiguity, suggesting that violations are acceptable as long as an attempt to avoid them is made. This reduces the protection afforded to intellectual property and makes it harder to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act (EEA), 18 U.S.C. § 1832, criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the explicit prohibition of violating intellectual property rights, the change creates uncertainty about the Receiving Party's obligations, potentially creating a loophole that could be interpreted as condoning actions that would otherwise be considered theft of trade secrets under the EEA. The original text better aligns with a duty to protect confidential information, whereas the new text opens the door to the receiving party taking minimal action to protect it.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses 'shall not violate,' creating a clear and enforceable obligation to protect the Disclosing Party's intellectual property rights. The modified language, 'should attempt to avoid violating,' introduces ambiguity and weakens this obligation. The Economic Espionage Act (EEA), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1832), criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the prohibition of violating intellectual property rights, the modified contract creates a loophole that could be interpreted as condoning actions that would otherwise be considered theft of trade secrets under the EEA. The change from a strict prohibition to a weaker, suggestion-based standard directly undermines the intent to protect trade secrets, creating a significant contradiction.
